Michael Jackson's innocence in child abuse allegations is supported by several factors, though opinions remain divided. 

1. Chandler case: Evan Chandler demanded $20M or a film deal from MJ before allegations; taped calls showed extortion intent, leading to a civil settlement without admission of guilt.

2. FBI files: Over 300 pages show no evidence of wrongdoing; they assisted local probes but found nothing incriminating.

3. 2005 trial: Acquitted on all 10 counts (molestation, conspiracy) after a 4-month trial; jury cited weak evidence and accuser inconsistencies.

4. Leaving Neverland inaccuracies include: timeline errors (e.g., Safechuck's train station abuse post-dates its build); prior sworn denials of abuse; impossible tour dates; misdated events; contradicted "honeymoon" story; financial motives (book deals); inconsistent family testimonies. Rebuttals argue memory lapses and grooming explain discrepancies.

Ultimately, no convictions, but some view allegations as credible.

MICHAEL just opened to $39.5 million on Friday. The biggest biopic opening day in history.

The film legally cannot show anything that happened to Michael Jackson after 1988.

The version of his life that arrived in theaters this weekend is the legally permissible portion. Lionsgate had already filmed a third act that dealt with the 1993 lawsuit when producers discovered a clause in the original settlement: the accuser had secured the right to never be depicted or mentioned in any film or television production. The third act got reshot. Original budget $155 million. Final cost closer to $200 million. The most expensive music biopic ever made, partly because they paid to film scenes they then paid to delete.

So the movie ends with the 1988 Bad tour. Which is what the image shows. Jaafar Jackson, Michael's actual nephew, in his first film role, performing on stage at a stadium concert recreation.

The casting decision runs on the same logic as the 1988 cutoff. The estate cast a Jackson because casting a Jackson is how you control the on-screen depiction at the bloodline level. The movie can't get made without estate cooperation because the estate owns the music. The estate gets veto over the actor by suggesting the actor.

Critics gave it 39% on Rotten Tomatoes. Audiences gave it A- on CinemaScore. The gap is structural. Critics review the screenplay. Audiences pay for the music and the moonwalk. Nostalgia is the most price-inelastic product in entertainment, and it doesn't show up in any review.

But the box office isn't the actual prize.

In February 2024, Sony bought half of Michael Jackson's recorded music and publishing catalog for at least $600 million, valuing the full asset at $1.2 billion. Largest single-artist music valuation in history. The estate retained creative control. Sony got the cash flow.

Every ticket buyer on Friday is a potential streamer on Saturday. The film functions as a 2-hour activation campaign for an asset Sony already owns half of, marketed by an estate that owns the other half.

Lionsgate spent $200 million making a movie. Sony got the biggest free promotional event in music catalog history. The estate turned a legal constraint into a marketing tentpole, paid for by a studio they don't own, to drive streaming revenue on a catalog they're partial owners of.

The real product was never the film.
